text: The surgeon positions the camera port supraumbilically, approximately ten centimeters above the umbilicus, due to the large size of the myoma. The right and left hand ports are placed 7.5 centimeters laterally from the camera port. A contralateral port is also positioned for the miami screw, which will be used by both the surgeon and assistant. The surgeon introduces a Veress needle using an ellipsoid number knife.

The surgeon makes a small 2mm incision at the supraumbilical point on the skin. A Veress needle is inserted perpendicularly to the abdominal wall and obliquely to the patient to introduce pneumoperitoneum. The irrigation and suction tests are performed, followed by the hanging drop test. Once confirmed, the insufflator tubing is attached to establish pneumoperitoneum, with pressure being monitored.

The surgeon removes the Veress needle after reaching the preset intra-abdominal pressure and enlarges the incision for the primary trocar. Using a two-hand technique, the primary trocar is inserted, followed by diagnostic laparoscopy. The second port is placed 7.5 centimeters lateral and below, and the third port is introduced. The port positions are crucial, with the right-hand port being 18 centimeters from the target umbilicus and the telescope positioned 24 centimeters away.

The surgeon introduces a 5mm port for the right-hand dissector, harmonic, or suction irrigation, positioned 7.5 centimeters lateral to the midline, just above the anterior superior iliac spine. Another port is placed medially for the left-hand myoma screw, with a contralateral port for assistance. The ports are now in position for the initial diagnostic phase of the laparoscopic myomectomy.

The laparoscopic view reveals the uterus completely occupied by a large myoma. Visible anatomical structures include the uterosacral ligament, left fallopian tube, round ligament, and left infundibulopelvic ligament. The ureter is observed below the infundibulopelvic ligament, with visible peristalsis. The sigmoid colon, cecum, and appendix are also identifiable in the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies anatomical landmarks including the triangle of doom, triangle of pain, round ligament, deep ring, medial and lateral umbilical ligaments, and inferior epigastric vessels on the right side. The pubic symphysis and median umbilical ligament are also visible. The left ovary and fimbria are noted, along with the IP ligament and uterine artery. Vasopressin is injected to induce transient ischemia, followed by the use of a myoma screw to hold the myoma. A horizontal elliptical incision is made to facilitate suturing and removal of the myoma.

The surgeon uses suction irrigation in the right hand and a myoma screw in the left hand to gradually separate the myoma from the surrounding muscle fibers. A second myoma screw is applied from the opposite side to assist in the separation. Muscle fibers on the right side are carefully enlarged to facilitate the separation process. The myoma screw is repositioned to optimize traction, allowing for effective enucleation of the myoma.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the myoma, fully detaching it from the uterine tissue. The myoma is visibly removed, leaving no remnants behind. The surgeon prepares to perform intracorporeal suturing using a needle holder through a 5mm port, demonstrating the technique for four-layer suturing.

The surgeon holds the inner muscle layer, preparing it for suturing. The assistant holds the suture tail to facilitate continuous suturing of the internal muscle layer, ensuring no serosa is included. The suture progresses from right to left, followed by suction irrigation to clear blood before returning the suture back for the second layer.

The surgeon completes the suturing of the muscular layer without involving the serosa, using a serger's knot for termination. The suturing is locked, and the zero muscular layer is addressed with continuous suturing using a Dundee jamming knot.

The surgeon uses suction to remove blood collected in the area. A Dundee jamming knot is applied to the seromuscular layer, starting posteriorly due to a small tear from an electrical incision on the serosa. The knot is self-locking, and continuous suturing of the seromuscular layer is performed.

The surgeon performs continuous suturing on the seromuscular layer, closing a vertical tear and starting horizontal suturing for the third layer. Irrigation and suction are used to clear blood for better visibility. The suturing reaches the left corner, where the assistant holds the suture for termination with the final fourth layer, which involves serosa-to-serosa approximation using a self-locking technique.

The surgeon uses the left hand to hold the suture while the right hand continues suturing with the needle. The assistant holds the needle end of the third layer of the suture, which is not yet tied. The final layer is being sutured, reinforcing the lower tier. Continuous suturing is performed, with intermittent locking. A deep bite is taken at the left corner to stop oozing, resulting in slight ischemia and a paler appearance at the corner. The needle is cut and removed to facilitate easier suturing. The third layer suture is terminated with the fourth layer suture, completing the secure four-layer closure.

The surgeon cuts the suture, confirming there is no bleeding, and the ovary appears intact. The suture is removed, and the process of morcellation begins, which involves gradually fragmenting the tissue for removal.

The surgeon completes the final stage of morselation, carefully removing all morselated tissue fragments using a stone-holding forceps. A finger is inserted through the 15 mm port to ensure complete removal. A cobbler's needle is used to pass and pull out the suture. The 15 mm port is closed to prevent hernia, while the two 5 mm ports remain open.
